content.text: Why Release Branches for GenAI

Most GenAI services are deployed as continuously running APIs rather than installable packages, which means a simple trunk-based "deploy from main" strategy is often sufficient. Release branches become necessary in two situations: when multiple customers or tenants run different versions of the service simultaneously, and when the regulatory or compliance context requires an auditable artifact (a named branch) that represents exactly what is running in a specific environment.

For teams that do not need either, release branches add coordination overhead without benefit — deploy from trunk and use feature flags instead. For teams that do need them, the practices below keep release branches from becoming a long-lived maintenance burden.

attrs.alt: Release branch strategy: trunk feeds release/1.2 and release/1.3 via cherry-pick; patches apply as hotfixes

content.text: When to Create a Release Branch
Situation
Use release branch?
Alternative if not
Single tenant, continuous deployment
No
Deploy from trunk; use feature flags for staged rollout
Multiple tenants on different contract versions
Yes
One release/N.M branch per supported version
Regulated industry requiring a versioned audit artifact
Yes
Branch name and tag become the artifact identifier
Staged environment promotion (dev→staging→prod)
No
Use the same trunk commit promoted through environments
Emergency rollback needed in under 2 minutes
No
Feature flags provide faster rollback without branch ops
Naming and Lifecycle

# Branch naming: release/<major>.<minor>
# - major: breaking changes to API or model behavior contract
# - minor: new capabilities, backward-compatible
# - patch: hotfixes only, never new features (delivered as tags on the release branch)

# Create a release branch from a specific trunk commit
RELEASE_SHA=$(git rev-parse HEAD)    # or any trunk commit
git checkout -b release/1.2 "$RELEASE_SHA"
git push -u origin release/1.2

# Tag the initial release
git tag v1.2.0 -m "Release 1.2.0: adds calendar tool integration"
git push origin v1.2.0
Lifecycle Rules
Never develop on a release branch:
all new work happens on trunk; release branches only receive cherry-picks
Support at most two release branches simultaneously:
supporting three or more makes cherry-pick coordination unsustainable
Close a release branch when its last tenant upgrades:
delete the branch and mark the tag as the final version
Minor versions get a new branch; patch versions get a new tag on the existing branch:
release/1.2 carries tags v1.2.0, v1.2.1, v1.2.2
Cherry-Picking Hotfixes to Release Branches
The standard flow for a production bug fix: fix on trunk first, then cherry-pick to affected release branches. Never fix on a release branch alone — that leaves trunk broken.

# Step 1: fix the bug on trunk (through normal PR process)
git checkout -b hotfix/tool-call-json-parse-error
# ... fix the bug ...
git commit -m "fix: handle malformed JSON in tool call response"
# → PR to main, CI passes, merged

# Step 2: identify the merge commit SHA on trunk
TRUNK_SHA=$(git log main --oneline | head -1 | awk '{print $1}')

# Step 3: cherry-pick to each affected release branch
git checkout release/1.2
git cherry-pick "$TRUNK_SHA"
# Resolve any conflicts (rare if branches are not diverged far)
git push origin release/1.2

# Step 4: tag the patch release
git tag v1.2.1 -m "Hotfix: handle malformed JSON in tool call response"
git push origin v1.2.1

# Repeat for release/1.3 if also affected
git checkout release/1.3
git cherry-pick "$TRUNK_SHA"
git push origin release/1.3
git tag v1.3.1 -m "Hotfix: handle malformed JSON in tool call response"
git push origin v1.3.1
Fix-on-trunk-first rule:

if the fix cannot be expressed as a clean cherry-pick onto the release branch, that is a signal the release branches have diverged too far. Consider upgrading tenants to the latest version rather than maintaining the diverged branch.
Eval Gates on Release Branches

A cherry-pick is a code change and must re-trigger the eval suite on the release branch. This is separate from the CI run on trunk — the model behavior on an older release branch may differ from trunk due to accumulated prompt and config differences.

# .github/workflows/release-branch-eval.yml
name: Release Branch CI

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - 'release/**'

jobs:
  eval-suite:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Detect release version
        id: ver
        run: |
          BRANCH="${{ github.ref_name }}"   # e.g. release/1.2
          VERSION="${BRANCH#release/}"       # e.g. 1.2
          echo "version=$VERSION" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Run eval suite for this release version
        run: |
          python scripts/run_evals.py \
            --suite "release-${{ steps.ver.outputs.version }}" \
            --min-pass-rate 0.90
        env:
          OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}

      - name: Tag as pre-release candidate
        if: success()
        run: |
          git tag "v${{ steps.ver.outputs.version }}-rc.${{ github.run_number }}"
          git push origin --tags

Maintaining a version-specific eval suite (release-1.2, release-1.3) rather than a single shared suite is necessary because older release branches may have different tools, different system prompt versions, and different expected behaviors.
Prompt and Config Versioning Across Releases
The prompt and tool config on a release branch may differ from trunk. Track these differences explicitly so cherry-picks do not silently change behavior.

# src/config/versions.py
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ReleaseConfig:
    version: str
    system_prompt_path: str
    tool_schemas: list[str]     # paths to enabled tool JSON files
    model: str
    max_tokens: int

RELEASE_CONFIGS = {
    "1.2": ReleaseConfig(
        version="1.2",
        system_prompt_path="src/prompts/system_v1_2.txt",
        tool_schemas=["src/tools/calculator.json"],
        model="gpt-4o",
        max_tokens=2048,
    ),
    "1.3": ReleaseConfig(
        version="1.3",
        system_prompt_path="src/prompts/system_v1_3.txt",
        tool_schemas=["src/tools/calculator.json", "src/tools/web_search.json"],
        model="gpt-4o",
        max_tokens=4096,
    ),
}

def get_config(version: str) -> ReleaseConfig:
    if version not in RELEASE_CONFIGS:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown release version: {version!r}")
    return RELEASE_CONFIGS[version]

When a cherry-pick modifies the system prompt, create a new versioned prompt file rather than overwriting the existing one. This makes the diff explicit and keeps the config for older releases immutable.
Deciding When to End Support for a Release Branch
Signal
Action
All tenants on this version have migrated to the next
Close the branch; delete it from the remote
The release branch has diverged so far that cherry-picks require manual resolution
Negotiate a migration deadline with the tenant; do not extend indefinitely
The underlying model version used by this release is being sunset by the provider
This is a hard deadline — work backward to set a migration date
Security vulnerability found that cannot be cherry-picked cleanly
Patch on the release branch directly (exception to fix-on-trunk-first) with a simultaneous trunk fix
Common Pitfalls
Treating release branches as development branches:
once a team starts committing new features to release/1.2 instead of trunk, the branches diverge permanently and become unmergeable
Forgetting to cherry-pick a hotfix to all affected branches:
use a checklist or automation to track which release versions are affected by each fix
No version-specific eval suite:
cherry-picks that pass the trunk eval can still break the release branch if the branch uses a different prompt version
Supporting too many versions:
supporting more than two simultaneous release branches is a sign that customers need a clearer migration path, not more branch maintenance
Automating the Cherry-Pick Workflow

Manual cherry-picks are error-prone when applied across two or more release branches. A script that reads a hotfix commit, identifies affected release branches, and opens PRs for each reduces the chance of missing a branch.

# scripts/backport_hotfix.py
"""
Usage: python scripts/backport_hotfix.py <trunk-sha> [--dry-run]
Opens a cherry-pick branch and PR for each release branch that is affected.
"""
import subprocess, sys, argparse

RELEASE_BRANCH_PATTERN = "release/"

def list_release_branches() -> list[str]:
    output = subprocess.check_output(
        ["git", "branch", "-r", "--list", f"origin/{RELEASE_BRANCH_PATTERN}*"],
        text=True
    )
    return [b.strip().removeprefix("origin/") for b in output.splitlines() if b.strip()]

def cherry_pick_to_branch(trunk_sha: str, release_branch: str, dry_run: bool) -> str | None:
    backport_branch = f"backport/{trunk_sha[:8]}-to-{release_branch.replace('/', '-')}"
    if dry_run:
        print(f"[dry-run] Would create {backport_branch} from {release_branch}")
        return None

    # Create backport branch from the release branch
    subprocess.check_call(["git", "checkout", "-b", backport_branch, f"origin/{release_branch}"])
    result = subprocess.run(["git", "cherry-pick", trunk_sha], capture_output=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"Cherry-pick conflict on {release_branch}:\n{result.stderr.decode()}")
        subprocess.check_call(["git", "cherry-pick", "--abort"])
        subprocess.check_call(["git", "checkout", "main"])
        subprocess.check_call(["git", "branch", "-D", backport_branch])
        return None

    subprocess.check_call(["git", "push", "-u", "origin", backport_branch])
    return backport_branch

def main() -> None:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("trunk_sha")
    parser.add_argument("--dry-run", action="store_true")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    release_branches = list_release_branches()
    print(f"Found release branches: {release_branches}")

    for branch in release_branches:
        backport = cherry_pick_to_branch(args.trunk_sha, branch, args.dry_run)
        if backport:
            print(f"Created {backport} — open a PR targeting {branch}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Model Version Pinning on Release Branches

A release branch fixes not only code but also the model version used in production. When an LLM provider releases a new model version, a release branch should not silently pick it up — the new model may behave differently even on identical prompts.

# src/llm_client.py
import os

# The model version is locked per release branch.
# To upgrade, create a new minor release (release/1.3) with the new model version.
#
# Do NOT read model from an environment variable that can be updated at runtime —
# that bypasses the branch-level audit trail.
_PINNED_MODEL = "gpt-4o-2024-08-06"   # updated at branch cut time, not dynamically

def get_model(override: str | None = None) -> str:
    if override and os.environ.get("ALLOW_MODEL_OVERRIDE") == "true":
        # Only in development — never set ALLOW_MODEL_OVERRIDE in production
        return override
    return _PINNED_MODEL
When a model version is sunset by the provider, that is a hard deadline for migrating tenants off the release branch that pins it. Track model sunset dates alongside tenant upgrade plans.
Release branch
Pinned model
Model sunset date
Action required by
release/1.1
Migrate before 2025-05-01
release/1.2
2026-Q1 (planned)
Migrate before 2025-12-01
release/1.3
Not announced
No action yet
Testing Release Branch Compatibility Before Tenant Migration

When a tenant is migrating from release/1.2 to release/1.3, run a compatibility eval before switching production traffic. The eval compares outputs from both versions on the tenant's actual usage patterns.

# scripts/compatibility_eval.py
"""
Compare model outputs between two release versions on a sample of real traffic.
Used before migrating a tenant to a new release branch.
"""
import asyncio, json, pathlib
from llm_client import chat
from config.versions import get_config

async def eval_single(input_text: str, old_version: str, new_version: str) -> dict:
    old_cfg = get_config(old_version)
    new_cfg = get_config(new_version)

    old_prompt = pathlib.Path(old_cfg.system_prompt_path).read_text()
    new_prompt = pathlib.Path(new_cfg.system_prompt_path).read_text()

    old_out, new_out = await asyncio.gather(
        chat(system=old_prompt, model=old_cfg.model,
             messages=[{"role": "user", "content": input_text}]),
        chat(system=new_prompt, model=new_cfg.model,
             messages=[{"role": "user", "content": input_text}]),
    )
    return {
        "input": input_text,
        "old": old_out.content,
        "new": new_out.content,
        "diverged": old_out.content != new_out.content,
    }

async def run_compatibility_eval(
    old_version: str,
    new_version: str,
    sample_inputs: list[str],
) -> dict:
    results = await asyncio.gather(*[
        eval_single(inp, old_version, new_version)
        for inp in sample_inputs
    ])
    diverged = [r for r in results if r["diverged"]]
    print(f"{len(diverged)}/{len(results)} inputs produced different output")
    return {"results": list(results), "divergence_rate": len(diverged) / len(results)}

# Run: python -c "
#   import asyncio, json
#   from scripts.compatibility_eval import run_compatibility_eval
#   inputs = json.loads(open('data/tenant-abc-sample.json').read())
#   result = asyncio.run(run_compatibility_eval('1.2', '1.3', inputs))
#   print(result['divergence_rate'])
# "
A divergence rate above 10% on a tenant's real traffic warrants a manual review of the diverging cases before completing the migration. Below 5% is typically safe to proceed with.
Hotfix Prioritization Across Multiple Release Branches
When a critical bug is discovered that affects both active release branches and trunk, triage before acting. Not every trunk fix needs to be backported immediately to every release branch.
Severity:

security vulnerabilities and data-loss bugs → backport immediately to all affected branches. Accuracy regressions → backport in the next scheduled patch. Cosmetic issues → backport only if the tenant requests it
Scope:
if the bug is in code that differs between trunk and the release branch, the cherry-pick may require adaptation. Flag this before committing to a timeline
Communication:
notify affected tenants when a backport is in progress; give them a patch tag they can deploy rather than asking them to pin to a branch commit
Patch tag before deploy:
always tag a patch version (v1.2.1) before directing a tenant to deploy the backport. Never tell a tenant to deploy a branch HEAD — branches move, tags do not
Release Branch Checklist
Use this checklist when cutting a new release branch to ensure the branch starts in a known-good state.
Trunk is green:
all CI checks pass on the trunk commit being branched from
Eval suite passes:
run the full eval suite against the branch commit before tagging v<major>.<minor>.0
Prompt and config versions are explicit:
system_prompt_path, tool_schemas, model, and max_tokens are recorded in ReleaseConfig for this version
Version-specific eval suite exists:
evals/release-<major>.<minor>/ directory is created with the relevant test cases
Model sunset date is noted:
the pinned model's known or estimated sunset date is recorded in the release tracking spreadsheet or issue tracker
Tenant onboarding documented:
the tenant's integration guide refers to v<major>.<minor>.0, not to the branch name
Previous release branch lifecycle reviewed:
confirm that the team is not exceeding the two-simultaneous-branch limit; plan a migration for the oldest branch if needed
Summary

Release branches for GenAI services are appropriate when multiple tenants run different contract versions simultaneously or when a versioned audit artifact is required. The core discipline is: all development happens on trunk, release branches only receive cherry-picks, fixes go to trunk first, and each cherry-pick re-triggers the release branch's eval suite. Limiting support to two concurrent release branches and maintaining version-specific prompt configs and eval suites keeps the coordination overhead manageable.
Default position:
if you can deploy from trunk and use feature flags, do that instead of release branches. Release branches solve a multi-tenant versioning problem, not a deployment velocity problem.
